items.title: Prior and posterior intuition
items.description: Bayes' theorem is the formal identity behind updating P(A) to P(A|B); the Prior/Posterior Intuition topic develops how priors, evidence, and posteriors behave across repeated observations.

items.title: Likelihood functions
items.description: The factor P(B|A) in Bayes' theorem is the likelihood of evidence B under hypothesis A; the Likelihood Functions topic treats it as a function of parameters given fixed data.

items.title: Naive Bayes assumption
items.description: Naive Bayes classifiers apply Bayes' theorem to compute P(class | features), assuming conditional feature independence to make the likelihood tractable.

items.title: Random variables
items.description: Events A and B are often defined on random variables (e.g. label Y, feature X); PMFs and PDFs from the Random Variables topic supply the probabilities plugged into Bayes' formula.

items.title: Maximum likelihood estimation
items.description: MLE picks parameters that maximize P(data|θ) without an explicit prior P(θ); Bayesian inference uses Bayes' theorem to obtain P(θ|data) instead.

items.title: Conditional probability definition
items.description: Bayes' theorem is derived from P(A|B) = P(A ∩ B)/P(B) and P(B|A) = P(A ∩ B)/P(A); understanding conditional probability as restricted sample space is prerequisite.
